coredata.dc:title: Optimal battery state of charge parameter estimation and forecasting using non-linear autoregressive exogenous
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Nefraoui, Amal
Kandoussi, Khalid
Louzazni, Mohamed
Boutahar, Abderrahim
Elotmani, Rabie
Daya, Abdelmajid
coredata.dc:description: The lithium-ion battery (LiB) has become the most widely used energy storage system for electric vehicles (EVs) due to its many advantages. The EV battery pack needs a battery management system (BMS) to estimate the state of charge (SOC) and balance the energy capacity through the cells. Apart from the fact that it is still challenging to accurately solve, the SOC forecasting represents an important concern in the study sector. This research proposes an effective battery SOC forecasting approach utilizing the non-linear autoregressive exogenous model (NARX) time’s series optimized Levenberg-Marquardt training algorithm, and Bayesian-Regularization (BR). The suggested technique is well-known for its resilience and high performance in nonlinear and complex system prediction, and it is extensively used in a wide range of disciplines. Also, the precision of the NARX technique has been investigated as a function of training data sets, error classifications based on experimental data of LiB. Both algorithms were evaluated with experimental data. Discharging followed by resting process was conducted on a 2.6 Ah LiB. They demonstrate good convergence in the low error and regression. In an effort to address a gap in the field, this paper offers a comparison between NARX-LM and NARX-BR algorithms for the LiB SOC prediction. Both algorithms are optimized the ANN using times series analysis based in the same training data. The results show that NARX-BR is more rapid and accurate with a low mean square error (MSE) of 2.39 10-5 than NARX-LM, which achieved an MSE of 1.11. Thus, it shows NARX-BR as an effective technique for LiB SOC prediction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Electric vehicles
State of charge prediction
Artificial neural network

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One resistance–capacitance branch electrical circuit battery model (Thevenin model).
Discharging-resting process results: Voltage (a) and Current (b) profiles.
Neural Autoregressive with exogenous input neural network block diagram.
Variation of the open circuit voltage and discharging voltage in time.
Discharging voltage and Open Circuit Voltage variation following SOC of 2.6Ah LiB.
NARX regression results for Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm between experimental and forecasted data.
NARX-Bayesian Regularization algorithm regression results between experimental and forecasted data.

head.ce:title: Optimal battery state of charge parameter estimation and forecasting using non-linear autoregressive exogenous

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Science Engineer Laboratory for Energy, National School of Applied Sciences, Chouaib Doukkali University of El Jadida, El Jadida 24000, Morocco

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chouaib Doukkali University of El Jadida

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Science Engineer Laboratory for Energy, National School of Applied Sciences, Chouaib Doukkali University of El Jadida, El Jadida 24000, Morocco

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Laboratory M3ER, FSTE, Moulay Ismail University, Meknes, Errachidia 509, Morocco
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Laboratory M3ER, FSTE, Moulay Ismail University, Meknes, Errachidia 509, Morocco

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The lithium-ion battery (LiB) has become the most widely used energy storage system for electric vehicles (EVs) due to its many advantages. The EV battery pack needs a battery management system (BMS) to estimate the state of charge (SOC) and balance the energy capacity through the cells. Apart from the fact that it is still challenging to accurately solve, the SOC forecasting represents an important concern in the study sector. This research proposes an effective battery SOC forecasting approach utilizing the non-linear autoregressive exogenous model (NARX) time’s series optimized Levenberg-Marquardt training algorithm, and Bayesian-Regularization (BR). The suggested technique is well-known for its resilience and high performance in nonlinear and complex system prediction, and it is extensively used in a wide range of disciplines. Also, the precision of the NARX technique has been investigated as a function of training data sets, error classifications based on experimental data of LiB. Both algorithms were evaluated with experimental data. Discharging followed by resting process was conducted on a 2.6 Ah LiB. They demonstrate good convergence in the low error and regression. In an effort to address a gap in the field, this paper offers a comparison between NARX-LM and NARX-BR algorithms for the LiB SOC prediction. Both algorithms are optimized the ANN using times series analysis based in the same training data. The results show that NARX-BR is more rapid and accurate with a low mean square error (MSE) of 2.39 10-5 than NARX-LM, which achieved an MSE of 1.11. Thus, it shows NARX-BR as an effective technique for LiB SOC prediction.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Long-short-term memory-weighted fading extended Kalman filtering

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, electric vehicles (EVs) have attracted more encouragement to be adopted into the transportation sector around the world because of their major advantage of zero carbon emissions. This technology requires an energy storage system, where the lithium-ion batteries are the most used as an energy storage device. It has several significant advantages over other technologies, including very high energy density, low maintenance, a longer life cycle, no memory effect, and a low self-discharge [1–3]. The battery consists of two electrodes (cathode and anode), an electrolyte, and a separator. During the charge/discharge process, lithium ions move from the cathode to the anode via a separator and a solvent [4,5]. They are made up of one or hundreds of cells that transform chemical energy directly into electrical energy. The cells can be connected in either series or parallel to acquire the appropriate current and voltage capability and assure the performance of the EV battery system.

During charge and discharge, the characteristics of the battery depend on its internal parameters, such as internal resistance, voltage, current, and capacity. Therefore, it is crucial to monitor and control these parameters to prevent damage to the battery and ensure its optimal performance. This can be achieved through the use of battery management systems (BMS) that regulate the charging and discharging processes and provide real-time monitoring of the battery's internal parameters. The BMS prevents batteries from overcharging and/or over-discharging by controlling cell balancing and energy management [7,22]. Also protects the system from dangerous accidents, rapid ageing, and equipment failure by accurately estimating a crucial parameter characterising the battery pack: the state of charge (SOC) that indicates the system’s remaining capacity [11].

Because of the chemical behaviour inside the battery, the SOC estimation is more complicated. As a result, a battery model should be developed to simulate the chemical and electrical behaviours [6]. In the literature, different models have been developed and implemented; the most widely used models in recent research are the electrochemical model and the electrical equivalent circuit model [7]. The electrochemical battery models are significantly more reliable but typically require a lot of system resources, making them unsuitable for many real-time applications. These models reflect the thermodynamic and kinetic behaviours that occur in the cell, using a series of coupled nonlinear differential equations [8,9]. It aids in accurate battery state prediction because it represents internal activities and physical behaviour within the battery, such as solid-phase diffusion and charge transfer procedures[2]. They necessitate point by point computations of the inner electrochemical process. Since this is a tough challenge, it takes a long time to respond and compute. For the time being, electrochemical parameterization is inappropriate for meaningful BMS applications [8–10]. The equivalent electrical circuit model (ECM) is employed in a variety of battery applications because of its rapid completion time, accessibility, and excellent accuracy [11,12]. The ECM seems to have inadequate model generalisation under a larger range of operating conditions when the battery is forced to meet its functional limitations. As a result, it is not a battery model that is frequently used for applications requiring high current rates or that are run at very low temperatures [13,14]. Excluding the electrochemical model and the ECM, there has been a growing tendency to integrate the two models to create further complex battery management algorithms that are operationally efficient and capable of indicating battery states and fault occurrences [15–18].

Several battery models have been used in practical applications to forecast operational voltage, power, and energy capacity to guarantee adequate quality and reliability [19,20]. Beyond everything, battery models are important for establishing quantitative algorithms for evaluating battery state, such as state of health (SOH) and state of charge (SOC), in operational rechargeable batteries [21]. In order to provide such a high-efficiency estimating approach that maintains steady battery performance, theoretical forecasting calculations are frequently implemented within an embedded BMS. It assures safety and enhances effectiveness during execution time in applications such as EVs by modelling implementation loads [22].

In the literature, several papers have used different methods to estimate and identify the SOC parameters. The existing methods are arranged into two main groups: model-based methods such as Open Circuit Voltage and Coulomb Counting methods, and data-driven based methods such as Artificial Neural Network Method (ANN) and Kalman Filter (KF)[13]. The open-circuit voltage (OCV) method is a nonlinear function of SOC that has commonly been employed [16]. The [17] proposes an OCV based method for capacity estimation, utilising iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS), for capacity identification at various ageing rates. The results showed good accuracy. However, it cannot be used for dynamic web application estimation since the storage system requires a long time of resting to reach a steady state [19,23]. On the other hand, the Coulomb counting methods (CC) are based on the integral of the current value over time. It is commonly used and has the advantage of online measurement and simple calculation, but unlike the OCV method, the measured quantity needs to be larger. And unfortunately, it accumulates small errors due to the integration of the current term within the working time, which makes it less accurate. A KF method has been used to describe the state and observation equations, where the SOC is the internal state and needs a specific ECM to generate the corresponding equation of the state. This method has numerous advantages, including the ability to self-learn, high precision, and real time calculation. But it cannot be used for a non-linear system. For instance, researchers have developed various techniques based on the derivative KF methods to fit the nonlinearity and dynamic behaviour of batteries [13]. For example, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is a recursive prediction filter based on recursive algorithms and state space techniques [24–28]. The Unscented Kalman filter (UKF) and dual EKF estimate the state of the system following the measured input and output values [23,27,29–31]. Adaptive square-root cubature Kalman filter, were also treated [32]. Caian et al. suggest in [33] the improved forgetting factor recursive least squares to identify the battery model variables on-line, and then, based on dynamically updated battery model parameters, they deploy the EKF for SOC estimation. However, in terms of convergence speed and calculation time, these techniques are still restricted.

Meanwhile, the Intelligent computation techniques such as Artificial Neural Network (ANN), inspired by the human brain [34–36], takes great interest in solving complex problems accurately and in a reasonable amount of time. It is known as a highly stable tool due to its massive parallel structure [37]. It doesn’t need a large number of parameters like the other methods mentioned before. In addition, it is not necessary to understand the ageing mechanism of batteries. It predicts using various data analysis and machine learning techniques based on data collection [38].

ANN has been used in various fields, including music education [39]. For its powerful algorithms that solve numerous non-linear systems using different numbers of neurons and architectures. It has a self-learning skill and relies on learning from experience data. The structure of ANN is divided into three layers: an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer interconnected with each other [40–42]. Each neuron network responds to time-series inputs differently depending on the weights that are determined during the training. Several studies have opted for ANN to predict the SOC for different battery technologies using different models and training algorithms (e.g.,optimised Levenberg-Marquardt wavelet neural network [43], load-classifying neural network [44], Deep neural networks [45]).

Other ANN-optimised methods were utilised for SOC estimation and forecasting as well. The improved feedforward-long short-term memory (FF-LSTM) modelling method is proposed in [46] to ensure the whole-life-cycle SOC prediction performance under DST working conditions by taking into account the variations in current, voltage, and temperature. Whang et al. proposed an improved steady state screening model for the predicted SOC redundancy reduction [46]. The long-term capacity decay results indicate that the battery's charging capacity decreases significantly with time. In [47], the authors used a Deep Neural Networks (DNN) model for the SOC estimation. Then the DNN model output is inputted into the long short-term memory (LSTM) model, which provides the SoC prediction. In [48], the author used an optimised long-short-term memory-weighted fading extended Kalman filtering (LSTM-WFEKF) model. An AI technique was fused with an enhanced filtering algorithm, in [49], and combined with a multi-hidden layer long short-term memory (MHLSTM) network with a suboptimal fading extended Kalman filtering (SFEKF) method, for SoC estimation. Although these optimised methods have been used for estimating and predicting LiB SOC with some success offering a low mean squared error, they are relatively complex and require significant computational resources, large amounts of data, and time for training and optimisation. Furthermore, they are very sensitive to the quality of the training data and process, making it difficult to select the characteristic hyper parameters, which cannot guarantee accurate SOC and SOH estimation under complex and real time applications.

Some studies have opted for data driven methods for state of health (SOH) estimation. The SOH of a battery refers to its current condition or degradation level compared to its original state when it was new. Ming Zhang et al. reviewed some powerful data driven algorithms for SOH prediction that offered good accuracy and performance results [50]. The SOH estimation can also be done simultaneously with the SOC estimation. The Cubature Kalman Filter (CKF) algorithm offered good estimation accuracy when combined effectively with the H-infinity algorithm for the co estimation method for SOC and SOH estimation [51]. Takyi-Aninakwa et al. co-estimated the SOC and the SOH of LiB considering different temperatures based on the Dual Extended Kalman Filter (DEKF) method. And to respond to the nonlinearity effect of temperature, the author suggests the ensemble empirical mode decomposition (EEMD) method with adaptive noise for original data extraction and decomposition [52]. This field warrants further attention, even though it is not our focus in this paper.

The forecasting techniques, according to the measured data variation within the time period, use time series and regression analysis, where NARX is considered the convenient type of ANN[53]. NARX learns based on historical data, feedback inputs, and additional exogenous time series to predict the time series of interest. The Levenberg–Marquardt and Bayesian algorithms for regulation have shown robustness and effectiveness in predicting highly nonlinear characteristics, such as solar radiation and wind speed [54]. Moreover, they provided precise estimates of photovoltaic power where the BR showed good accuracy while the LM offered rapid performance[55]. NARX has proven high SOH estimation precision with untrained data [56].

The purpose of this research is to use the NARX method, a nonlinear technique using an autoregressive exogenous input based neural network known for its exceptional accuracy, quick training, and interference tolerance. Trained using two optimization algorithms: the LM algorithm and the BR algorithm. This method is proposed in [57] and has concluded that BR showed high accuracy under complete charging and discharging conditions for LiB SOH estimation, and in [58], the NARX was trained using three algorithms; LM algorithm, BR algorithm and Scaled Conjugate Gradient Algorithm (NARX-SCGA),where the BR outperformed photovoltaic power forecasting. Although, as far as we are aware, none of the previous research has compared the two algorithms for the same experimental dataset for LiB SOC prediction. Thus, this paper also offers a comparison between BR and LM algorithms with experimental data. For further accuracy, statistical measures are generated to examine their performance. The results show high alignment with the experimental data with small errors, and the BR algorithm performs better than the LM algorithm in terms of mean square error (MSE) and regression rate (R).

The using of the NARX optimized by LM and BR are based in the experimental data measured in the Science Engineer Laboratory for Energy, National School of Applied Sciences at El Jadida, Morocco. The battery, which had a full charge, was powered by the solar panel, which also served as a storage device for the data. The battery was then drained with a continuous current for 5 min before being placed in an open circuit to obtain the steady state voltage. The acquisition of the experimental data uses the data acquisition card, relay, Arduino hardware and software, Li-ion battery and load.

Therefore, this study is structured as follows: Section 3 introduces the battery model considered, and Section 4 goes into the experiment. Section 5 focuses on the prediction of LiB SOC using two training algorithms, Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) and Bayesian-Regularisation (BR). Section 6 includes the results and discussion of experimental and simulation results. Lastly, Section 7 goes over the conclusion of our work.

Accurate mathematical and equivalent electrical circuit models of any system are essential for analysis, simulation, prediction, and monitoring to present the electrical and dynamic behaviours. In the research area, several models have been presented and analysed using different methods and techniques to present the characteristic curves and predictions of the batteries. A LiB SOC is used to estimate its parameters and forecast the discharging behaviour using experimental material and the NARX method. In this section, an electrical equivalent circuit and mathematical modelling of LiB SOC are presented, along with a detailed description of the used materials, parameter estimation, and forecasting.

Fig. 1 depicts the Thevenin equivalent electrical circuit of the battery, which reflects the battery’s static and dynamic behaviour. The optimal voltage source for the open circuit voltage (OCV) model is one that is adequate to the battery's state of charge. The ohmic resistance describes the battery’s internal resistance. The parallel Resistor capacitor network (Rp and Cp) depicts the battery’s dynamic behaviour generated by interfacial charge-transfer processes at the electrode [9]. The time constant for the RC pair is represented by the combination of Rp and Cp used to simulate battery polarisation effects. A significant series of experiments has demonstrated that using a first order RC equivalent circuit model is more appropriate for its simplicity and high accuracy [59]. In this paper, the first order RC ECM is considered and used to model parameter estimation and SOC forecasting.

The open circuit voltage (OCV), which is mostly influenced by the battery's state of charge (SOC) [60]. The SOC characteristics curve is represented by a nonlinear equation as VOC = a + b*SOC in the SOC intervals, where a and b are constants. Additionally, the battery SOC value, which is between 0 and 100%, may well be obtained using the standard Coulomb counting techniques [25,61].

The experimental data are measured using Lithium-polymer battery (LiPo), 2600 mAh, 7.4 V implemented and used with different materials, and the measurement experimental data are analysed. The LiPo is commonly used in the electrical vehicle, due for their small size, lightness and high performance. It is constituted by two cells connected in series, and has a charging rate of up to 2 C and a discharging rate of up to 30 C. Fig. 3 illustrates the used material and composed from photovoltaic simulator, Arduino Mega, LiPo Battery and load.

We are focused on predicting the discharging behaviour of the battery; initially, the battery was fully charged (SOC = 100%), by adopting a photovoltaic panel simulator (PV) as charging source. Then discharged for 5 min using a constant current, and putted in open circuit until it reached the steady state voltage. This process was repeated until the battery was completely discharged (SOC = 0%). The experience was controlled using an Arduino Mega microcontroller, which was linked to a computer through the USB port and programmed using Arduino software to simply upload the relevant code and control the status of a relay that links the LiPo to the load. Fig. 2 shows the measured current and voltage pulse discharge data used in this investigation. As shown in Fig. 3 , the experimental setup includes a LiPo battery, a PV simulator, a relay, a load to imitate motor usage, and a data gathering the instrument.

The battery state of charge SOC defined as the amount of stored electrical energy in the battery, as shows the equation (6), where

Q 0

The artificial neural network (ANN) model is founded on the human brain structure. This computational model works the same way as nerve cells. Across the biological environment, the neuron collects and combines a range of signals from other inputs. Each symbol other neuron is just in charge of fundamental computations. In the meantime, the technique’s consequences are often nonlinear, and the output is the end outcome. In the context of the research domain, ANNs have been called data-driven models and are composed of two distinct components termed input and output: the first receives data, whereas the second is a hidden layer that exposes the generated final results [63,64]. The hidden layer continuously acquires knowledge, it remembers, and builds correlations amongst output and input data. The ANN training procedure can frequently be linked by adjusting the weights of each link. ANN also features neurons that are linked to each other in different layers of the network. Nodes are the name for these neurons, interconnected by weights that may be adjusted in accordance with the quality metrics that assess the closeness between the desired response and the one generated. In the scientific field, all researchers agreed on the significance of the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) the desired results. The ANN has been identified as among the most commonly utilized artificial intelligence technology for forecasting in a variety of applications [65].

Thus, the aim of our work is to predict the LiB SOC using ANN, time series analysis optimized using LM and BR algorithms. The model chosen is the nonlinear autoregressive with exogenous input (NARX). Because it presents the most practical model in dynamic system prediction and has the ability to predict the future value according to the given current and previously saved values of the supplied time series data. It offers best correlations of capacity data tracking [43], as illustrated by the block diagram in the Fig. 4 . The output equation of this network is shown in (8), with;
f

represents the training function,
x
(

)
is the input time series discharge voltage and current of the LiPo,
y

)
indicates the output time series yet is the SOC, and d corresponds to the delay parameter.
(8)

( x

, y

⋯ . , y
t - d
)

The NARX network can be trained using a variety of functions. Levenberg Marquardt (LM) and Bayesian regularization (BR) are the ones selected in this study. To achieve a successful network design, it is important to choose the most influential database, number of neurons and delays.

The LM algorithm was developed for optimising solutions to nonlinear least squares problems. It has been used in numerous regression and curve-fitting tasks for parameter estimation. Generally, least-squares problems appear in the context of fitting a certain parameterized mathematical model to a set of data points, which is often experimental based data. This method consists of adjusting the parameters, such as weight and bias, of a nonlinear model iteratively in order to minimise the difference between the model's predictions and the observed data [66,67]. It is an adjustable method that interpolates between the Gauss-Newton algorithm (GNA), which approximates the Hessian matrix (second-order partial derivatives) of the cost function using only the first-order derivatives (Jacobian matrix), and the Gradient-Descent method (GDM), meaning It uses the gradient (first-order derivative) of the cost function to determine the update direction [68,69]. When the damping parameter is set to a small value, the LM algorithm behaves more like the GNA, prioritising the rapid convergence seen in GNA. On the contrary, when the damping parameter is set to a large value, the algorithm behaves similarly to the GDM. To ensure stability and avoid convergence issues, smaller steps towards the optimal are taken [70]. The mathematical equation of LM algorithm following the Eq. (9) [71].
H =
J ′
. J
(9)
x
k + 1
=
x k
-
J

- 1

′
. ε
where J is the Jacobian matrix,
x
k+1 is Hessian matrix,
x
k is the weight vector, I is the identity matrix, µ the scalar operator and the ɛ is the residual vector.

The algorithm consists of successively approximating the initial parameter values to the desired local. The training set is divided into validation and test vectors. Validation vectors are used to end training early if the network's performance is not improving. Test vectors are utilised to guarantee the generalisation of the network. Once the maximum number of repetitions is attained, the performance gradient decreases under a certain threshold [72].

The BR is identified as one of the best training methods for solving learning problems due to its capacity to randomly select regularisation coefficients and the fact that it combines high-convergence qualities. It significantly improves estimation accuracy by incorporating a limitation concept into the NN’s training requirements component in order to guarantee the best configuration of the network’s weight connection. The BR principle consists of defining prior distributions over model parameters, which are specified for the NN weights and biases, and updating them based on observed data. This enables the model to balance between fitting the data and keeping the previous assumptions [73,74].
(10)

is the sum of the squares of the network weights,

is neural network connection weights, M is the number of connection rights and α, β are the regularisation parameters.

To predict the LiPo SOC and verify the accuracy of the implemented NARX model-based; we employed two neurons for the input layer related to LiB’s current and voltage, which influence the SOC. The output layer is one neuron which is the predicted SOC. And to test the accuracy of the developed NARX model, a varied number of hidden layers and delays was tested.

The total SOC data estimated, in the previous section was divided into three target time steps: 70% for training in which the network is adjusted based on its error, 15% for validation used to measure generalization, and 15% for testing that provides a separate performance measurement of the network without having any effect on the training.

The performance indicators are important for evaluating the NARX model once it has been trained. In this work, coefficient of determination (R2) and mean squared error (MSE) are opted to evaluate the accuracy of NARX prediction. These metrics are determined using the equations (12) and (13) respectively.
(12)
R 2

where N is the number of data points,
Y i

Y i
are the target and the real value, respectively, and
Y
¯
is the mean observed and predicted value. For good convenience between the predicted and true targets, it is necessary to have the MSE value close to zero and R2 close to 1 for better precision.

To predict the SOC and to validate the concordance of the LM and BR methods, it is important to estimate the actual values from the experiment. For this purpose, we used the coulomb counting method based on equation (1). Figs. 5 and 6
show the discharge voltage and OCV curves.

The LM algorithm showed good convergence for ten nodes and two delays; afterwards, the training MSE continued to decrease while the validation MSE increased, significantly higher than the training MSE; referring to an over fitting, so the 10 is the accurate number of neurons, where the training MSE is 2.903
10
- 28
and R2 is 1, the validation MSE is 1.11 and R2 is 1, then the testing has MSE and R2 values equal to 0.6751 and 1, respectively. The regression plot in Fig. 7 , reports the best fitting of the training data with the forecasted output. The regression coefficient approves the absence of any deviation from the original experimental values. When the generalization stops progressing, the training stops at a low MSE value, for LM training algorithms it shows good efficiency in the performance plot presented on a log scale, as seen in Fig. 8 , with 1.11 at 5 epochs as the best validation performance. The Fig. 9 shows a few generalization errors, scaled between −1 and 1.5.

On the other hand, the BR algorithm offered good results for nine hidden layers and two delays, with a training MSE = 2.3938
10
- 5
and R = 1, and a testing MSE = 1.0878
10
- 2
and R2 = 1, as highlighted in Fig. 10 . It approves the accordance between output and target, and points out the best fit with the actual data. And, as can be seen in the time series response in Fig. 11 , this training algorithm model maintains low error values: −0.15 for testing and less than 0.05 for training. Furthermore, according to Fig. 12 , it indicates the best performance at epochs 305 with 2.3938
10
- 5
.

Thereupon, comparing the two training algorithms, the BR shows better results; the mean square value is closer to zero than the LM algorithm-based method (
MSE
BR
<
MSE
LM
). Which means the BR training algorithm offers good training and provides better accuracy and best fitting of training data from predicted output.

This paper presents a comprehensive study on the electrical circuit-based model of lithium ion battery, focusing on parameter extraction from discharging curves. We obtained valuable insights into the LiB behaviour, by analysing the discharging profile consisting of 5 min discharge followed by 2 min rest until reaching 0% state of charge (SOC).

Furthermore, we suggest the use of the nonlinear autoregressive exogenous method (NARX) to predict the state of charge, employing two training algorithms Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) and Bayesian-Regularization (BR). Both algorithms demonstrate good results in forecasting the Lib SOC. For instance, NARX proves to be a simple and effective model based for the non-linear system.

Moreover, this paper evaluates the accuracy of the opted algorithms, which we presume to be absent in literature for Lib SOC prediction, where the BR algorithm offered better precision than the LM algorithm, based on MSE.

Overall, these results highlight the potential of the electrical circuit-based model and the NARX approach for battery analysis and prediction. The accurate performance of the BR algorithm indicates its capacity for accurate and rapid SOC forecasting.

Nefraoui Amal: Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Resources, Writing – original draft. Kandoussi Khalid: Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Visualization. Louzazni Mohamed: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Boutahar Abderrahim: Writing – review & editing. Elotmani Rabie: Investigation, Visualization. Abdelmajid Daya: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Louzazni, D. T. Cotfas, and P. A. Cotfas, ‘Management and Performance Control Analysis of Hybrid Photovoltaic Energy Storage System under Variable Solar Irradiation’, Energies, vol. 13, no. 12, Art. no. 12, Jan. 2020, doi: 10.3390/en13123043.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. I. Wahyuddin, P. S. Priambodo, and H. Sudibyo, ‘State of Charge (SoC) Analysis and Modeling Battery Discharging Parameters’, in 2018 4th International Conference on Science and Technology (ICST), Aug. 2018, pp. 1–5. doi: 10.1109/ICSTC.2018.8528631.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mapping of daylight illumination levels using global solar radiation data in and around Istanbul, Turkey
Weather

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Durna and A. D. Şahin, ‘Mapping of daylight illumination levels using global solar radiation data in and around Istanbul, Turkey’, Weather, vol. 75, no. 1, pp. 19–25, 2020, doi: 10.1002/wea.3386.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Artificial neural networks applications in building energy predictions and a case study for tropical climates
Int. J. Energy Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yalcintas and S. Akkurt, ‘Artificial neural networks applications in building energy predictions and a case study for tropical climates’, Int. J. Energy Res., vol. 29, no. 10, pp. 891–901, 2005, doi: 10.1002/er.1105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-term global solar radiation prediction in 25 cities in morocco using the FFNN-BP Method
Front. Energy Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Belmahdi, M. Louzazni, M. Akour, D. T. Cotfas, P. A. Cotfas, and A. El Bouardi, ‘Long-Term Global Solar Radiation Prediction in 25 Cities in Morocco Using the FFNN-BP Method’, Front. Energy Res., vol. 9, 2021, Accessed: Aug. 09, 2022. [Online]. Available: https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fenrg.2021.733842

Sustain. Energy Technol. Assess.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Louzazni, H. Mosalam, A. Khouya, and K. Amechnoue, ‘A non-linear auto-regressive exogenous method to forecast the photovoltaic power output’, Sustain. Energy Technol. Assess., vol. 38, p. 100670, Apr. 2020, doi: 10.1016/j.seta.2020.100670.

Eng. Appl. Artif. Intell.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Micev, M. Ćalasan, D. Stipanović, and M. Radulović, ‘Modeling the relation between the AVR setpoint and the terminal voltage of the generator using artificial neural networks’, Eng. Appl. Artif. Intell., vol. 120, p. 105852, Apr. 2023, doi: 10.1016/j.engappai.2023.105852.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: H. P. Gavin, ‘The Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm for nonlinear least squares curve-fitting problems’, 2022.

ISA Trans.

Numer. Math.

IEEE Trans. Neural Netw.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A two-step combined algorithm based on NARX network and the subsequent prediction of the residues improves prediction accuracy of the greenhouse gases concentrations
Neural Comput. Appl.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Buevich, A. Sergeev, A. Shichkin, and E. Baglaeva, ‘A two-step combined algorithm based on NARX neural network and the subsequent prediction of the residues improves prediction accuracy of the greenhouse gases concentrations’, Neural Comput. Appl., vol. 33, no. 5, pp. 1547–1557, Mar. 2021, doi: 10.1007/s00521-020-04995-4.

E3S Web Conf.
